coredata.dc:title: Amphiphilic polyurethane-assisted uniform dispersion of TiO2/carbon dots hybrids in amidoximed polyarylene ether membranes for enhanced oil/water separation and photocatalytic organic dye degradation

coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic oil–water separation membranes are critical for addressing water pollution and energy sustainability, but their widespread application is hindered by membrane fouling and limited photocatalytic efficiency, primarily caused by hydrophobic substrates and photocatalysts aggregation. To overcome these challenges, we designed a novel membrane integrating amphiphilic polyurethane (PU) and hydrophilic TiO2/modified carbon dots (MCD) into a functional polyarylene ether amidoxime (PEA) matrix via non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS). The TiO2/MCD photocatalysts exhibited a reduced bandgap of 2.86 eV and uniform dispersion with an average particle size of 40 nm. The optimized PEA-PU-3T/M membrane possessed a hierarchical pore structure with a maximum porosity of 84.7 % and enhanced surface hydrophilicity with a water contact angle of 22°. Furthermore, the resultant membrane demonstrated a water flux of 968 L·m−2·h−1 and 98.8 % emulsion separation efficiency. Importantly, its photocatalytic performance achieved 94.2 % and 99.1 % removal rates for methylene blue and rhodamine B, respectively. These enhanced performances were attributed to the amphiphilic PU-assisted uniform dispersion of hydrophilic TiO2/MCD hybrids, as well as the adequate interaction between the hybrids and the functional PEA. Overall, this study offers valuable insights into the development of innovative photocatalytic oil–water separation membranes for the remediation of complex wastewater containing dyes and oils.

head.ce:title: Amphiphilic polyurethane-assisted uniform dispersion of TiO2/carbon dots hybrids in amidoximed polyarylene ether membranes for enhanced oil/water separation and photocatalytic organic dye degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic oil–water separation membranes are critical for addressing water pollution and energy sustainability, but their widespread application is hindered by membrane fouling and limited photocatalytic efficiency, primarily caused by hydrophobic substrates and photocatalysts aggregation. To overcome these challenges, we designed a novel membrane integrating amphiphilic polyurethane (PU) and hydrophilic TiO2/modified carbon dots (MCD) into a functional polyarylene ether amidoxime (PEA) matrix via non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS). The TiO2/MCD photocatalysts exhibited a reduced bandgap of 2.86 eV and uniform dispersion with an average particle size of 40 nm. The optimized PEA-PU-3T/M membrane possessed a hierarchical pore structure with a maximum porosity of 84.7 % and enhanced surface hydrophilicity with a water contact angle of 22°. Furthermore, the resultant membrane demonstrated a water flux of 968 L·m−2·h−1 and 98.8 % emulsion separation efficiency. Importantly, its photocatalytic performance achieved 94.2 % and 99.1 % removal rates for methylene blue and rhodamine B, respectively. These enhanced performances were attributed to the amphiphilic PU-assisted uniform dispersion of hydrophilic TiO2/MCD hybrids, as well as the adequate interaction between the hybrids and the functional PEA. Overall, this study offers valuable insights into the development of innovative photocatalytic oil–water separation membranes for the remediation of complex wastewater containing dyes and oils.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: A large amount of oily wastewater pollution caused by industrial and textile sewage have a tremendous impact on human health and ecological system, due to difficulty with spontaneous degradation [1,2]. Fortunately, the membrane separation technology, with its advantages of easy operation, high efficiency and low energy consumption, is considered a promising strategy for wastewater treatment [3,4]. Compared with inorganic membranes (ceramic [5], carbon nanotubes [6], and Cu3(PO4)2 [7]), polymer separation membranes (polyvinylidene fluoride [8], polysulfone [9] and polyacrylonitrile [10]) are characterized by easy manufacturing, good flexibility and convenience of modification, which have been widely used in wastewater treatment [11]. However, actual oily wastewater exhibits diverse and complex characteristics, often consisting of oil-in-water emulsions containing surfactants and dyes [12]. Single-functional oil–water separation membranes are inadequate for meeting the complex requirements of wastewater treatment. Therefore, membranes with multi-functionalities are urgently needed to simultaneously remove oil and other pollutants.

Photocatalytic separation membranes are an efficient and environmentally friendly multifunctional materials [13]. They not only enable oil–water separation performance, but also demonstrate significant advantages in pollutant degradation and self-cleaning property, as they can effectively degrade organic pollutants to harmless substance (CO2 and H2O) [14]. These features allow them to have extensive applications in environmental protection. For instance, Ma et al. [15] developed polyimide nanofiber membranes based on a superlipophilic and superhydrophobic ZIF-8@GSH composites with photocatalytic properties using electrospinning method, enabling effective oil–water separation and dye degradation. Xu et al. [16] successfully fabricated a NRGT-PSF photocatalytic composite membrane via NIPS method, in which the NRGT nanocomposites improved the photocatalytic performance and separation of the primary PSF membrane. By incorporating various photocatalysts into polymer matrixes, these resultant photocatalytic membranes have achieved great advancement in treating complex oily wastewater [17,18]. However, these photocatalytic membranes exhibited poor resistance to contaminations due to the hydrophobic nature of the membrane matrix. Consequently, their surface was easily contaminated with oil, resulting in distinctly decreased separation efficiency during prolonged use [19]. In order to solve this issue, a large number of hydrophilic photocatalytic membranes have been developed in recent years. Most reported hydrophilic photocatalytic membranes are primarily prepared by grafting polymer brushes onto the surface of membrane substrates, or incorporating hydrophilic nanoparticles (such as Al2O3, TiO2, ZnO and Ag) with polymer matrixes [20]. Despite of the improved hydrophilicity to some extent, however, these membranes often suffer from tedious modification procedures, or weak bonding between the hydrophilic filler and the hydrophobic substrate, causing filler detachment and uneven dispersion of the filler, resulting in insufficient hydrophilic modification [21]. Notably, the inadequate interaction between hydrophilic photocatalysts and hydrophobic polymer substrate also lead to the self-accumulation of photocatalysts, resulting in the limited photocatalytic performance [22,23]. This negatively impacts the anti-pollution ability and photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the membranes, significantly impeding their practical application in long-term and efficient treatment of oily wastewater with co-existing dyes pollutants [24,25]. Therefore, developing a new kind of multifunctional composite membrane with synergistically enhanced hydrophilic and photocatalytic properties through a facile protocol to accomplish long-lasting and high-efficient oil–water separation and dye degradation is highly desired yet remains challenging.

Through molecular design, we synthesized a novel functional copolymer, polyarylene ether amidoxime (PEA), which incorporated both amidoxime (–NH-C(=N-OH)-) and carboxyl (–COOH) functional groups. This was achieved by partial amidoximation of the nitrile groups in polyarylene ether nitrile (PEN). The introduction of these reactive moieties in PEA not only allowed its strong interactions with other functional modifiers, but also improved the hydrophilicity of the base membrane. Additionally, an amphiphilic polyurethane (PU) and photo-catalytically active TiO2 modified carbon dots (MCD) hybrid particles were synthesized as modifiers. The composite membranes were fabricated by a one-step nonsolvent induce phase separation (NIPS) process, in which PEA, PU and TiO2/MCD hybrids were mixed together. Among them, the intermolecular interactions between amphiphilic PU and PEA with abundant functional groups as well as TiO2/MCD hybrids, not only enhanced the hydrophilicity of the composite membranes but also promoted the uniform dispersion of TiO2/MCD hybrids. Thus, the obtained PEA-PU-T/M composite membranes exhibited improved oil–water separation performance, excellent photocatalytic ability and, more importantly, desired stability and reusability in the treatment of complex dye-containing oil–water emulsions. Schematic illustration of preparation of PEA-PU-T/M membranes and separation mechanism are shown in Scheme 1 .

The raw material of polyarylene ether nitriles (PEN, Mw = 50,000) was synthesized via a nucleophilic substitution polycondensation. 2,6-difluorobenzonitrile (DFBN, AR) was purchased from Yangzhou Tianchen Fine Chemical Co. Bisphenol A (BPA), hydroxylamine hydrochloride (NH2OH·HCl), N-Methylpyrrolidone (NMP), toluene, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium carbonate (K2CO3), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), hydrochloric acid (HCl), titanium dioxide (TiO2), N, N-dimethylacetamide (DMAc, AR) and butane-1,4-diol (BDO) were obtained from Cologne Chengdu. Isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI, 99 %), 2,2-bis (hydroxymethyl) propionic acid (DMPA) and polyethylene glycol-2000 (PEG-2000) were purchased from Macklin. O-phenylenediamine (OPD) and aluminum chloride hexahydrate (AlCl3·6H2O) were obtained from Acros Organics. All the chemicals used in the experiment were used directly without further purification.

The PEA random copolymers bearing pendent carboxyl and amidoxime groups were synthesized via a one-step nucleophilic substitution polycondensation reaction and a post-modification amidoxime reaction (Scheme 2 ). In the synthesis step, monomers of BPA (22.828 g, 0.10 mol) and DFBN (13.979 g, 0.105 mol) and catalyst of K2CO3 (20.73 g, 0.15 mol) were added into a flask containing mixed solvents of NMP (55.2 mL) and toluene (18.4 mL). The reaction was carried out under ambient atmospheric conditions in a sealed reactor. Initially, the reaction mixture underwent dissolution and dehydration via thermal treatment at 140 °C for 3 h. Then, the reaction system gradually heated to 180 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C·h−1 through precisely regulating toluene outflow (5–10 mL·h−1). After that, the temperature was held at 180 °C for 2 h to enhance degree of polymerization. Reaction completion was confirmed by two criteria: (1) stabilization of polymer viscosity, indicating termination of chain propagation, and (2) the emergence of the Weissenberg effect, characterized by viscoelastic rod-climbing behavior of typical polymer solutions under shear stress. Then, the reaction mixture was transferred to a beaker containing deionized water for 24 h to obtain the precipitated crude product, which was pulverized into powders and subjected to secondary purification as well as dried to obtain the purified polyarylene ether nitriles (PEN). For the amidoxime transformation process, the purified PEN (10 g) powders and NH2OH·HCl (12 g) were firstly dissolved in hot NMP solvent (100 mL). Then, NaHCO3 and NaOH were added to adjust the pH of solution to weak alkaline, followed by the reaction at 80 °C for 24 h to obtain the PEA crude product, which was washed repeatedly with 0.05 mol/L HCl, ethanol and H2O, and finally dried at 80 °C for 48 h to obtain pure PEA powders. It is worth noting that both the PEN synthesis process and the amidoxime transformation process can be conducted in medium-sized reactors (30 L), and PEN has been successfully mass-produced on an industrial scale.

Amphiphilic polyurethane (PU) was synthesized through a two-step process. Initially, PEG-1000 was dehydrated by heating to 100°C under vacuum for 4 h. In the pre-polymerization step, dewatered PEG-1000 (20 g) was mixed with IPDI (8.89 g) and reacted at 60 °C under nitrogen for 3 h, with DBTDL catalyst added to facilitate the reaction. For chain extension, the mixture was diluted with 10 mL acetone, followed by the sequential addition of DMPA (1.61 g) and BDO (0.72 g). The reaction proceeded at 60 °C under nitrogen for an additional 3 h, yielding the amphiphilic PU.

The preparation of the modified carbon dots (MCD) was based on the previous report [26]. Specifically, the synthesis involved a hydrothermal treatment of AlCl3·6H2O and OPD powders (1:7 mass ratio). The homogenized mixture was sealed in a Teflon-lined autoclave, heated at 200 °C for 6 h to activate precursor, and then ramped to 240 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min for 6 h to induce crystallization. After reaction finished, the product was pulverized and sequentially washed with deionized water (3×) and ethanol (2×), centrifuged (12,000 rpm, 5 min), and finally vacuum-dried (80 °C, 12 h) to obtain the purified modified carbon dots (MCD). To obtain TiO2/MCD hybrids, the desired amount of TiO2/MCD were dispersed in a mixture of 10 mL of DMAc and 20 mL of water and sonicated for 1 h. Subsequently, the mixed solution was transferred to a 200 mL hydrothermal reactor and reacted at 200 °C for 3 h. Finally, TiO2/MCD hybrids were obtained by washing three times with deionized water, followed by drying at 120 °C for 6 h. After drying, they were ground into fine powder for use, abbreviated as T/M.

The PEA-PU-T/M composite membranes were formed by nonsolvent induce phase separation (NIPS). Firstly, a certain proportion of TiO2/MCD hybrids and PU were dispersed in DMAc solvent, subjected to magnetic stirring for 30 min, followed by ultrasonic dispersion for an additional 30 min to achieve varying concentrations of dispersions. Subsequently, a certain proportion of PEA, LiCl and DMAc were mixed, then dissolved under high-speed stirring at 30 °C, until a mixture solution was obtained. The incorporation of LiCl into the casting solution induced a pronounced salting-out effect, where dissociated Li+ ions attenuated polymer intermolecular interactions, thereby enhancing the polymer dispersion efficiency [27]. Then, T/M−PU−DMAc solution was added into the above mixture solution with continuous stirring until into a homogeneous solution. After ultrasonication for 1 h to eliminate bubbles, a homogeneous cast film solution was obtained. This solution was then cast onto a clean glass plate, propelled via a 200 μm wiper at a consistent rate and exposed to air for 30 s. Finally, the system was subsequently immersed in deionized water for 24 h to facilitate solvent-non-solvent exchange, during which the water was refreshed for every 8 h, to obtain polyurethane/polyarylene ether amidoxime membranes with photocatalytic properties (PEA-PU-T/M). The as-prepared separation membranes should be stored long-term in water before using. More details of the additive ratios of raw materials are shown in Table 1 .

The thermal behavior of polyarylene ether amidoxime (PEA) composites was characterizedby thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, TA Q-50) under an air atmosphere and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC, TA Q-100) under a nitrogen atmosphere. Both measurements were conducted at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. Functional groups and elemental states of membranes were identified via Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS50R) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo, K-Alpha). Morphological evaluation was performed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL, JSM 6490 LV) for topographical imaging and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker AFM Dimension Icon) for nanoscale roughness quantification. AFM measurements were conducted in non-contact mode with a scan rate of 0.9 Hz and an amplitude setpoint of 115.06 mV. Crystalline structure and porosity of TiO2/MCD and membranes were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and gas adsorption analysis, while contact angle measurements were carried out to assess the wettability of membranes. Photocatalytic dye degradation efficiency of composite membranes was monitored in real-time using UV–vis spectroscopy (TU-1810). Hydroxyl (•OH) and superoxide (•O2 −) radicals generated during the process were confirmed via electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, Bruker EMXplus-6/1) using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) as spin-trapping agents. EPR measurements were performed at 9.85 GHz under 1.000 G, and the details provided in Supplementary material (Text S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of oil-in-water emulsion separation performance
ce:section.ce:para: The separation performance of PEA-PU-T/M membranes for oil-in-water emulsions was quantitatively assessed by flux and separation efficiency tests. Model O/W emulsions were prepared by homogenizing isooctane, isopropanol, hexane and dodecane (100 ppm hydrocarbon content) with sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate (SDS) stabilizer in a 9:1 oil-to-surfactant ratio prior to pressurized filtration experiments. Separation flux (J) was calculated via Eq. (1).

Separation efficiency (Re) was calculated via Eq. (2).

where Vx (L) is the permeate volume of the water or O/W emulsions, A (m2) is the effective membrane area of 1.8376 cm2, and ΔT (h) the filtration time. C0 and Cf (mg·L-1) are the oil concentration of original O/W emulsion and the collected filtrate, respectively. For each cycle of the separation experiments, a 10 mL oil-in-water emulsions was freshly prepared and used.

To evaluate the antifouling performance of a membrane quantitatively, the flux recovery ratio (FRR), total pollution ratio (Rt), reversible pollution ratio (Rr), and irreversible pollution ratio (Rir) were crucial parameters. The formulas for these parameters were as follows Eq. (3)-(6):

where J1 and J2 are the first pure water flux through the membrane and the pure water flux after filtering the O/W emulsion, respectively, and Jp is the flux of the O/W emulsion.

Methylene Blue (MB) and Rhodamine B (Rh B) were chosen as the organic pollutants for the photodegradation test. The UV lamp (100 W, 365 nm) was used to provide the stable UV light irradiation. The PEA-PU-T/M membranes with 2.5 cm diameter discs were placed in 60 mL of MB and Rh B solutions (20 ppm), respectively, and stirred in the dark at 25 rpm for 30 min to achieve adsorption–desorption. After adsorption–desorption equilibrium was reached, the UV lamp, placed with 10 cm from the solution surface, was turned on for 60 min to proceed photodegradation process.

The concentrations of MB and Rh B were determined by UV–vis spectrophotometer for different irradiation times. The degradation efficiency (η) of the samples was calculated by Eq. (7):

where C and C0 represent the concentration of the solution at equilibrium and the concentration of the MB and Rh B solution before equilibrium, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Composition analysis of PEA, PU and TiO2/MCD hybrids
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to verify that the resultant polyarylene ether amidoxime (PEA) was successfully synthesized, the FT-IR spectrum was implemented to analyze the chemical composition of PEA. As shown in Fig. 1
a, typical double peaks at 3482 cm−1 and 3381 cm−1 were attributed to the O-H and N-H stretching vibrations of PEA. The peak attributed to the stretching vibration of nitrile group (CN) at 2231 cm−1 was weak, while the overlapping peaks of CN and CO at 1670 cm−1 was obvious, which represented that some of the CN was converted to amidoxime group and carboxyl group [28]. In addition, the benzene ring stretching vibration peaks on the main chain of PEA were detected in the range of 1457 cm−1–1578 cm−1. Also, the symmetric vibration peak and the asymmetric peak of Ar-O-Ar at 1017 cm−1 and 1239 cm−1 were also found, indicating that the backbone structure was not destroyed after the transformation process from polyarylene ether nitriles (PEN) into PEA. Furthermore, a clear exothermic spike appeared in the DSC curve of PEA at about 217 °C (Fig. 1 b), and the exothermic enthalpy was calculated as 82.59 J/g, which also manifested that PEN was successfully converted into the PEA with reactive carboxyl group and amidoxime group in the side chain. More interestingly, the TGA curve of PEA in Fig. 1 c revealed a distinct thermal decomposition stage in the temperature range of 200 °C to 325 °C, which is well known as the stable temperature for PEN, further confirming the PEA containing more reactive groups was acquired. Meanwhile, the temperature at 5 % heat loss (T5%) of PEA was detected to be 425 °C, and the char yield at 700 °C could reach 35 %, which indicates that the synthesized PEA still has good thermal stability and maintains the rigid backbone containing benzene rings.

In order to further verify that the prepared PEA was the expected structure, the PEA was characterized by XPS. The XPS full spectrum confirmed the presence of C, N, and O elements ( Fig. S1 ). For the C1s spectrum, the peak at 286.1 eV corresponds to C-N/C-O bonding, aligning with amine- and oxygen-containing moieties [29] (Fig. 1 d). For the O1s spectrum, the peak at 533.7 eV was attributed to C=O group, a characteristic signature of carbonyl or carboxyl group in polymer [30] (Fig. 1 e). Notably, the N1s spectrum displayed two peaks at 400.2 eV and 399.4 eV for −C(NH)2=NOH group and N-H/C≡N group, respectively, consistent with nitrogen-rich polymers [31,32] (Fig. 1 f). These results demonstrate the successful conversion of PEA. Additionally, the chemical structure of the amphiphilic polyurethane (PU) was characterized by FTIR spectrum, as demonstrated in Fig. 1 g, the PU displayed a broad absorption peak at 3518 cm−1 and 3321 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH and –NH bonds. The absorption peaks at 1699 cm−1, 1535 cm−1, 1239 cm−1, and 1094 cm−1 corresponded to the vibrational modes associated with C=O in the carboxyl group, carbamate bonds(–NHCOO-), C-O in the carboxyl group and C-O-C in the hydrophobic chain stretching vibration respectively [33]. These above results indicated the successful synthesis of PU. As shown in Fig. 1 h, the weak absorption peak at 3567 cm−1 could be observed, which corresponded to the stretching vibration of the –OH group on TiO2. The broad peaks of T/M between 3120 cm−1 and 3610 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibrations of –NH on MCD and hydrogen-bonded –OH/–NH between MCD and TiO2 [34,35]. In addition, CO/CN stretching at 1622 cm−1 and primary amino C-N stretching at 1410 cm−1 were detected, and these peaks were attributed to MCD with π-bond structure [26]. It is noteworthy that the O-Ti-O characteristic peaks in TiO2 appeared at 805 cm−1 and 477 cm−1, while those in TiO2/MCD red-shifted to 800 cm−1 and 472 cm−1, respectively, revealing the existence of hydrogen bonding interaction between TiO2 and MCD. Additionally, the microstructure of the materials was thoroughly analyzed using SEM. As shown in Fig. 1 i, TiO2 had a diameter of about 50 nm with a rough spherical surface, while MCD particles measured approximately 10 nm. At high magnification of SEM, it can be seen that small particles presented on the spherical surface of TiO2, illustrating the successfully combined of TiO2 and MCD [34]. On a macroscopic scale, the TiO2/MCD composite appeared as a blue powder. The successful preparation of these raw materials was confirmed by various characterization techniques, laying the foundation for the preparation of ultrafiltration membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology and structure of PEA-PU-T/M composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
PEA-PU-T/M composite membranes were facilely fabricated through evenly mixing PEA, PU and TiO2/MCD in DMAC solvent, followed by one-step NIPS process. Membrane morphology and surface structure are crucial parameters for assessing the role of membrane in O/W emulsion permeation and rejection. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis is essential for evaluating these morphological characteristics. The surface and cross-section micro-morphologies of pristine PEA membrane, PEA-PU and PEA-PU-T/M composite membranes were characterized by SEM images. As shown in Fig. 2
a, the unmodified PEA membrane exhibited sparse and small pore structures (10–20 nm) with an irregular cracked surface, which negatively impacted the separation efficiency and selectivity of the membrane. Additionally, the membrane showed a dense cross-sectional structure, which generally contributes to the low permeation (Fig. 2 b). Interestingly, the incorporation of PU into the PEA membrane resulted in an increase in both the size and number of the surface macropores, with the pore diameters ranging from 30 to 40 nm and the elimination of surface cracks ( Fig. 2 c). The cross-sectional images of the PEA-PU membranes show a characteristic asymmetric structure, with a dense top layer, a sublayer containing finger-like pores, and a cavity-like macroporous support layer (Fig. 2 d). These results indicated that PU could function as a porogen modifier to effectively enhance both the pore size and surface porosity of the PEA membrane. This effect is likely due to the hydrophilic segment of the amphiphilic PU, which easily migrated into the coagulation bath, promoting the formation of additional pores and increasing overall porosity. Meanwhile, PU enhanced the molecular chain flexibility of the system, compensating for membrane surface defects possibly caused by the slow movement of rigid chain segments during the molding process.

Notably, the incorporation of hydrophilic TiO2/MCD further significantly enlarged the membrane’s pore structure to 30–50 nm and resulted in the finger-like sublayer gradually evolving into macro-void pores that became irregular and curved (Fig. 2 e and Fig. 2 f). This morphology change is similar to previously reported work [36]. This structure variation is likely due to the synergistically increased hydrophilicity of the TiO2/MCD, amphiphilic PU to the membrane surface, resulting in the formation of a highly porous structure [37]. It is worthy noted that the enlarged porous structure and increased pore connectivity are key factors in enhancing membrane permeability [38]. As shown in Fig. 2 c-d and Fig. S2 , all membranes with incorporated PU exhibited a rough, wavy surface texture, which could be attributed to the migration of hydrophilic segments of PU chain to the membrane surface, followed by the deposition onto the PEA membrane, thereby altering its surface morphology by forming fibrous-shape PU structure [38,39]. This process caused localized phase separation of PU from the hydrophobic substrate, ultimately increasing the surface roughness of membranes [40,41]. The surface roughness of PEA, PEA-PU, and PEA-PU-3T/M membranes was quantitatively characterized via atomic force microscopy (AFM). As shown in Fig. S3
(a-d), the average mean roughness (Ra) values progressively increased from 37 nm (pristine PEA membrane) to 155 nm (PEA-PU membrane) and further to 176 nm (PEA-PU-3T/M membrane). Also, root-mean-square roughness (Rq) followed a similar increasing trend with values of 45 nm, 203 nm and 220 nm for pristine PEA, PEA-PU and PEA-PU-3T/M, respectively. This enhancement is possibly attributed to the interfacial interactions and hybrid-induced morphological modifications caused by incorporating amphiphilic PU and TiO2/MCD hybrids [42]. Specifically, the pristine PEA membrane exhibited a smooth surface morphology. Upon incorporating PU, phase-separated structures formed, which increased the surface roughness. Furthermore, the integration of TiO2/MCD hybrids introduced surface heterogeneity, leading to a further rise in roughness. These results quantify the positive contribution of PU and TiO2/MCD hybrids to the enhancement of membrane roughness. To further assess the pore architecture of the membranes, the porosity and average pore size were quantified using the traditional gravimetric technique. Fig. 2 g demonstrates that the porosity and average pore diameter of the composite membranes incorporated PU and TiO2/MCD with hydrophilic groups, significantly exceeded those of the pristine PEA membrane. This enhancement is attributed to the altered kinetics and thermodynamics of membranes formation during NIPS process, particularly accelerating the exchange rate of solvent and non-solvent [43–45]. These results are consistent with the above SEM images, in which the increscent pore diameter of PEA-PU and PEA-PU-3T/M at the finger-like pore tips and the proliferation of transverse pores within the pore walls contributed to the augmented porosity and average pore size of the membranes (Fig. 2 c-f). Furthermore, an excess of TiO2/MCD content led to a decline in porosity and average pore diameter, and 3 % mass fraction of TiO2/MCD was verified as the optimized usage amount values. This result may be ascribed to the formation of superabundant hydrogen bonds between PU and TiO2/MCD with concentrations surpassing 3 %, elevating the viscosity of the casting solution and impeding exchange rate of the DMAc solvent and water, and consequently leading to reduction in porosity and average pore size of the membrane [46]. The optimized PEA-PU-3T/M membrane possessed a hierarchical pore structure with a maximum porosity of 84.7 %.

Membrane hydrophilicity significantly influences its permeability and antifouling characteristics. A hydrophilic surface facilitates the formation of a hydration layer by interacting with water molecules, producing a barrier that impedes oil-polymer substrate interactions, thereby preventing fouling [47]. Herein, the water contact angles of pristine and modified PEA membranes were measured. As shown in the Fig. 2 h, the pristine PEA membrane exhibited poor hydrophilicity property with a WCA of 89° in air. With the addition of PU, the contact angles of PEA-PU decreased to 69°. After further being added with 3 wt% TiO2, the PEA-PU-3T membrane was obtained and exhibited a lower contact angle (61°). Notably, the introduction of PU and 3 wt% TiO2/MCD hybrids further improved the hydrophilicity of PEA membrane, and the contact angle of the resultant PEA-PU-3T/M membrane further decreased to 41°. With the concentration of TiO2/MCD hybrids increasing to 5 wt%, the PEA-PU-5T/M membrane acquired the lowest contact angle of 37°, and the PEA-PU-7T/M membrane with 7 wt% TiO2/MCD hybrids demonstrated a slightly enhanced contact angle (39°). After 20 s, the contact angles of PEA-PU-3T/M, PEA-PU-5T/M and PEA-PU-7T/M membranes decreased to 22°, 18° and 21°, respectively. These results indicate that the addition of both PU and TiO2/MCD hybrids can improve the wetting performance of PEA membranes, which can be ascribed to the increased surface roughness and the presence of numerous hydrophilic groups on these membranes surface containing the hydrophilic PU and TiO2/MCD hybrids, facilitating the absorption and rapid diffusion of water droplets on the membrane. The chemical characterization of the PEA composite membranes was carried out using FTIR spectroscopy and the results are shown in Fig. 2 i. The spectrum shows two broad absorption peaks near 3400 cm−1 in the PEA membranes, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of the –OH and –NH bonds within the PEA structure. In contrast, the PEA-PU and PEA-PU-3T/M membranes exhibited a larger and broader peak formed by the merging of these two peaks. This change is attributed to hydrogen bonding interactions between the PU chain segments and the –NH groups in amide of PEA [48]. Additionally, two distinct peaks at 1081 cm−1 for PEA merged into a single peak with a red-shift in the PEA-PU and PEA-PU-3T/M membranes. The observed change is likely due to the interaction between the ester-ether C-O-C groups in the PU chain and the aryl ether (Ar-O-Ar) groups in the PEA chain, which restricts the C-O-C stretching vibrations [49,50]. Besides that, the O-Ti-O absorption peak of TiO2/MCD hybrids at 472 cm−1 red-shifted to 462 cm−1 in the PEA-PU-3T/M membrane, which could be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonding between the active –COOH in PU and –OH on TiO2/MCD surface, thereby mitigating van der Waals forces between nanoparticles and promoting their uniform dispersion in polymer matrix. In short, PU and TiO2/MCD can be evenly interacted with PEA through hydrogen bonding interaction, which simultaneously enhanced the porosity and hydrophilicity of PEA membranes. In addition, when compared to the maximum stress of pristine PEA membrane (1.35 MPa), PEA-PU-3T/M showed a significantly increasing maximum stress of 2.60 MPa. ( Fig. S4 ) These results further indicated PEA with abundant functional groups could effectively interact with PU and TiO2/MCD hybrids to accomplish the elevated mechanical strength of resultant composite membranes.

The permeability, separation performance and antifouling performance of the membranes were evaluated using a dead-end filtration device with isooctane as the foulant. As shown in Fig. 3
a, it was evident that amphiphilic PU modified PEA membrane exhibited a significant increase of the water flux (up to 613 L·m−2·h−1), which was approximate higher than that of the unmodified PEA membrane. After further being added with 3 wt% TiO2, the PEA-PU-3T membrane was obtained and exhibited a higher water flux to 720 L·m−2·h−1. Notably, the introduction of PU and 3 wt% TiO2/MCD hybrids further improved the hydrophilicity of PEA membrane, and the water flux of the resultant PEA-PU-3T/M membrane further increased to 968 L·m−2·h−1, which was approximate ninefold higher than the water flux of the pristine PEA membrane. With the addition of different amounts of TiO2/MCD, it should be noted that the water flux of PEA-PU-T/M membranes decreased from 968 L·m−2·h−1 to 700 L·m−2·h−1 with the increasing content of TiO2/MCD from 3 wt% to 7 wt% in PEA-PU membrane. These results indicate that the addition of PU and TiO2/MCD additives increased the membrane pore size, porosity and surface hydrophilicity. This allowed more water molecules to enter the membrane, reducing the mass transfer resistance of water molecules in the pores. Additionally, the hydrophilic segments of the PU could attract and capture more water molecules into the membrane matrix, resulting in higher water permeability [45].

Furthermore, Fig. 3 b illustrates the O/W separation emulsions fluxes and rejection of these membranes, which were significantly lower than the water flux, although they exhibited a similar trend to the water flux. This result can be attributed to the formation of a filter cake layer due to the continuous precipitation and aggregation of trapped oil droplets on the membrane surface during the emulsion separation process. Specifically, compared with the PEA membrane with the emulsion flux of 22 L·m−2·h−1, the modified PEA-PU and PEA-PU-3T membranes exhibited increased fluxes to 99 L·m−2·h−1 and 168 L·m−2·h−1, respectively. Notably, synergistic addition of PU and different concentrations TiO2/MCD endowed the resultant PEA-PU-T/M membranes with more significantly increased emulsion flux, in which PEA-PU-3T/M membrane demonstrated the optimal emulsion flux of 280 L·m−2·h−1 compared to PEA-PU-5T/M (244 L·m−2·h−1) and PEA-PU-7T/M membranes (230 L·m−2·h−1). These results are consistent with the porosity and average pore size of the membrane in Fig. 2 i, indicating that the pore size structure has an important influence on the permeability of the membrane. Additionally, the rejection rate of pristine PEA membrane was 45.6 %, while that of PEA-PU (81.3 %) and PEA-PU-3T (85.9 %) composite membranes increased above 81.0 % and other three modified PEA membranes containing both PU and TiO2/MCD maintained rejection rates above 95.5 %. Particularly, PU-3T/M membrane achieved optimal performance with a retention rate exceeding 98.8 %. In addition, the separation performance of optimized PEA-PU-3T/M membrane for other hexane-in-water and dodecane-in-water emulsions were further investigated. As shown in Fig. S5 , the PEA-PU-3T/M membrane exhibited a flux of 175 L·m−2·h−1 for the hexane-in-water emulsion and 93 L·m−2·h−1 for the dodecane-in-water emulsion, with a rejection rate of approximately 86.4 % for the hexane-in-water emulsion and 96.3 % for the dodecane-in-water emulsion. These results indicated the PEA-PU-3T/M membrane possessed superior separation performance for isooctane-in-water emulsion, compared to other emulsions including isooctane and hexane.

After that, the antifouling performance of these membranes was systematically investigated. Flux recovery ratio (FRR), total fouling ratio (Rt), reversible fouling ratio (Rr) and irreversible fouling ratio (Rir) are commonly employed as a crucial characteristic parameter to evaluate the antifouling performance. Generally, a higher FRR and a lower Rt indicate better contamination resistance of the membrane [51]. As shown in Fig. 3 c, the pristine PEA membrane exhibited a FRR of 37.6 %, while incorporation of PU improved the FRR of PEA-PU membrane to 80.0 %. Additionally, the incorporation of 3 wt% of TiO2/MCD into PEA-PU membrane increased its FRR to 94.1 %. Further increasing the concentration of TiO2/MCD hybrids did not show significant change of the FRR of these composite membranes. The acquired high FRR values was mainly due to the good hydrophilic property of the membranes, which led to the formation of a hydration layer, thereby reducing the interaction between the membrane surface and oil pollutant to improve the FRR values. The total fouling ratio (Rt) of PEA-PU-3T/M was 50.5 % with 42.3 % Rr and 8.3 % Rir as compared to 76.7 % Rt with 32.4 % Rr and 44.8 % Rir for pristine PEA membrane. The significantly lower Rir of the former indicated effective inhibition of irreversible fouling during the O/W emulsion separation process under the synergistic effects of PU and TiO2/MCD. On the one hand, the hydrophilic chain segments of PU allowed the membrane surface with abundant hydrophilic –OH groups, which could form a thin hydration layer on the composite membrane surface through hydrogen bonding interaction with water. The hydration layer is generally believed to render the adhesion of oil droplets near the membrane surface in a thermodynamically unstable, prompting spontaneous return of oil droplets to the emulsion, and finally achieving a stable state [51]. On the other hand, the extra addition of hydrophilic TiO2/MCD further strengthened the hydration layer, which prevented irreversible interactions between contaminants and the PEA substrate, thereby promoting self-cleaning of contaminant molecules and effectively resisting membrane fouling by oil droplets. However, when more TiO2/MCD (such as 5 wt% and 7 wt%) was added to the PEA-PU membrane, appeared to irreversible fouling and a slight attenuation of Rir. This phenomenon could be primarily stemmed from two interrelated mechanisms. Firstly, excessive TiO2/MCD hybrids increased the viscosity of the casting solution, slowing the rate of solvent-nonsolvent exchange during the phase inversion process. This results in a dense substructure (pore size < 50 nm) and a thin, mechanically fragile skin layer. Under operational pressure, the brittle skin layer was susceptible to fracturing, which made the substructure directly contact to pollutant and narrow pores also caused contaminants to build up on the surface [52]. Secondly, the hydrophilic surface of TiO2/MCD particles enhanced their hydrogen bonding with polar contaminants (e.g., −SO3- in Rh B), resulting in the formation of a compressible gel layer. This layer dynamically densified during cyclic filtration, increasing hydraulic resistance (Rir) and irreversibility [53].

Furthermore, the emulsion separation efficiency was characterized using an optical microscope. As shown in Fig. 3 (d-f), the digital photos and optical microscope images of the emulsion feed and filtrate separated with the PEA-PU-T/M membranes show a significant difference. Specifically, the emulsion feed appears milky, with oil droplets of various sizes clearly visible. However, no visible oil droplets are found in the filtrate, indicating successful separation of the oil (isooctane) droplets from the emulsion.

Thanks to the widespread use and harmfulness of Methylene Blue (MB) and Rhodamine B (Rh B), they were chosen as a simulated pollutant for photodegradation performance investigation of the resultant membranes. Herein, one piece of membrane was immersed in MB aqueous solution, which was first stand in the dark for 30 min and followed by exposure under UV light for 60 min. As shown in Fig. 4
a, the change in MB concentration (C/C0) during this process was recorded under dark and UV light, and the reduction of simulated solution concentration in the dark due to the adsorption of MB onto the membranes were observed. Specifically, after being placed in the dark for 30 min, the C/C0 of MB for PEA membrane exhibited a slight decrease to 0.966, which represented the adsorption rate of the PEA membrane was only 3.4 %. Notably, the adsorption rates for PEA-PU, PEA-PU-3T, PEA-PU-3T/M, PEA-PU-5T/M, and PEA-PU-7T/M membranes were calculated as 11.7 %, 23.2 %, 33.6 %, 40.5 %, and 45.7 %, respectively. The higher adsorption of PEA-PU-(3/5/7) T/M membranes may be attributed to the rougher surface structure, larger porosity, and the enhanced adsorption of MB by PU and TiO2/MCD. Then upon the UV light, the concentration of the MB solution decreased within 60 min. The PEA and PEA-PU membranes depicted only minimal concentration changes due to the absence of photocatalytic activity. The PEA-PU-3T membrane with additional TiO2 exhibited the relatively limited MB degradation efficiency of 50.5 %. However, as the incorporation of different concentration of TiO2/MCD hybrids into the PEA-PU membrane, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the resultant PEA-PU-T/M membranes increased a lot and exhibited excellent photocatalytic degradation performance. Specifically, under the UV light irradiation for 60 min, the C/C0 ratios for PEA-PU-3T/M, PEA-PU-5T/M and PEA-PU-7T/M membranes were 0.127, 0.112, and 0.058, corresponding to degradation efficiencies of 87.3 %, 88.8 %, and 94.2 %, respectively. Furthermore, a comparison of PEA-PU-3T/M with PEA-PU-3T demonstrated that MCD, with its conjugated π-π bond structure, acts as an electron acceptor, effectively suppressing electron-hole recombination in TiO2. The bandgap of TiO2 sensitized with MCD decreased by 2.4 eV, extending its light absorption range and enhancing photocatalytic activity [54] ( Fig. S6 ).

The UV–vis spectra of the photodegradation process for all samples with varying TiO2/MCD contents were shown in Fig. S7 . The characteristic peak of MB at 665 nm gradually flattened as the TiO2/MCD contents increased. The synergistic effect of PU and TiO2/MCD not only enhanced the adsorption capacity, but also endowed the membrane with photocatalytic properties. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 4 b, the adsorption and photodegradation of Rh B by these prepared membranes exhibited a pattern similar to that of MB. Notably, after only 40 min of photoirradiation, the photodegradation efficiencies of the PEA-PU-3T/M, PEA-PU-5T/M and PEA-PU-7T/M membranes reached 96.2 %, 98.0 %, and 99.1 %, respectively. These results indicated that the PEA-PU-T/M membrane exhibited superior degradation performance for Rh B compared to that for MB dye. This is because Rhodamine B (Rh B) contains chromophoric groups such as (−C=C-/-C=N+), which contribute to a simpler molecular structure. These conjugated systems within Rh B are highly reactive and more easily oxidized by hydroxyl radicals (•OH) generated during the photocatalytic process. The structure of the chromophore in Rh B is less sterically hindered by bulky neighbouring groups, enhancing its exposure to reactive species such as hydroxyl radicals (•OH), consequently making it more susceptible to degradation [55,56]. In contrast, Methylene Blue (MB), a phenothiazine derivative, has a more complex chromophoric structure. The larger and more rigid molecular structure of the chromophore of MB, which includes the phenothiazine ring (C6H4-N=C) and auxiliary groups (–NH2, -S-), enhances its stability and reduces its susceptibility to oxidative attack [57,58]. As a result, MB demonstrates greater resistance to photodegradation compared to Rh B, leading to lower photodegradation efficiency. Moreover, the PEA-PU-T/M membranes could effectively photocatalytic degrade Methyl orange and Congo red dyes under 60 min UV irradiation, and the results are shown in Fig. S8 . Notably, after 2 h of sunlight exposure, the PEA-PU-3T/M, PEA-PU-5T/M, and PEA-PU-7T/M membranes achieved degradation rates of 89.2 %, 93.0 %, and 95.7 %, respectively ( Fig. S9
a-b). These results indicate PEA-PU-T/M membranes also demonstrated desirable visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation for Rh B dye.

Considering that the PEA-PU-3T/M membrane containing 3 wt% TiO2/MCD hybrids exhibited balanced performance in both oil–water separation efficiency and dye photodegradation capability, it was chosen as the optimal membrane for further studies. Under UV light exposure, the PEA-PU-3T/M membrane mediated the generation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and singlet oxygen (1O2), as detected by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy with spin trapping agents (DMPO for •OH, TEMP for 1O2). As shown in Fig. 4 c-d, the peak signals of DMPO-•OH and TEMPO-1O2 were observed in the EPR spectra, which were produced by TiO2/MCD after UV illumination. These results confirm that •OH and 1O2 were the primary reactive species generated during the dye photodegradation process under UV exposure. Moreover, the DMPO-•OH signal exhibited a maximum amplitude of 0.170 × 103 a.u., whereas the TEMPO-1O2 signal had a maximum amplitude of 0.025 × 103 a.u., reflecting the higher steady-state concentration of hydroxyl radicals due to their longer half-life under the experimental conditions [59]. Under UV excitation, photoelectrons and electron vacancies were produced from TiO2, while the MCD with a conjugated π-π bond structure, served as an electron acceptor, effectively suppressed the recombination of electron-hole pairs generated by TiO2 [60]. This action reduces non-radiative energy loss during the photocatalytic reaction, thereby improving photocatalytic efficiency of the membrane. The higher intensity of the central line observed in Fig. 4 d could be attributed to the generation of the superoxide radical (•O2 −), whose EPR signal overlapped with the central line of TEMPO, leading to a higher peak intensity at the center than at the sides, thus confirming the presence of superoxide radicals [59,61]. Subsequently, photoinduced electrons and holes engaged in redox reactions with water and oxygen molecules, producing highly reactive hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide anions (•O2 −). Some of the •O2 − radicals were further oxidized by valence band holes or trapped charges, leading to the formation of singlet oxygen (1O2) [62]. The photocatalytic degradation of pollutants by the PEA-PU-T/M membranes under illumination was illustrated through a series of equations (Fig. 4 e). Contaminants adsorbed both on the membrane surface and within its pores underwent efficient degradation via an advanced oxidation process involving •OH, •O2 −, and 1O2, resulting in the self-cleaning of the membrane while preserving the integrity of the composite membrane structure [63]. The introduction of PU and TiO2/MCD enhanced the hydrophilicity of the PEA membrane, facilitating the adsorption of water molecules onto its surface and providing an adequate reaction medium for the photocatalytic process. This includes hydrolysis to generate hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and singlet oxygen radicals (1O2), which in turn enhanced catalytic efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of PU on the photodegradation ability of composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
Previous studies have identified the enhancement effect of TiO2/MCD hybrids on the photodegradation ability of PEA-PU-T/M membrane. In order to further clarify the significance of PU in the composite membrane, a series of experiments were conducted. Firstly, the optical images and fluorescence spectra of TiO2/MCD solutions before and after addition of PU were acquired as shown in Fig. 5
a. The TiO2/MCD solution containing PU (T/M−PU) exhibited a clearer red hue and enhanced fluorescence intensity compared to the dark red TiO2/MCD solution (T/M). It is noted that the as-prepared MCD has been found to exhibit aggregation-induced-emission (AIE) property [26]. Hence, the color and fluorescence intensity variations suggested that PU can facilitate the dispersion of TiO2/MCD hybrids. Besides that, a new PU-free PEA-3T/M membrane was prepared as a control sample to reveal the role of PU in composite membranes. XRD analysis was performed to investigate the crystallography of the membranes. As depicted in Fig. 5 b, the TiO2 peaks in the TiO2/MCD composite were observed between 28° and 60° at 2θ values of 29.5°, 43.3° and 57.4°, corresponding to the crystal planes (101), (200), and (105), respectively [64]. The diffraction peak at 8.3° was attributed to MCD. Two broad diffraction peaks between 15° and 25° were observed, which belong to the PEA membrane [65]. Besides, a sharp peak around 28.5°, attributed to the (101) plane of TiO2, was founded in both XRD patterns of PEA-3T/M and PEA-PU-3T/M membranes. However, a weak peak at 8.2° originated from MCD was detected only in the PEA-PU-3T/M membrane. The peak was obscured in PEA-3T/M membrane, which is possibly due to the uneven distribution of TiO2/MCD, resulting in a low diffraction intensity. In the SEM image of Fig. 5 c, TiO2/MCD in the PEA-PU-3T/M membrane was uniformly dispersed as spheres. In contrast, TiO2/MCD hybrids in the PEA-3T/M membrane formed aggregates with an average edge length of approximately 100 nm (Fig. 5 d). Additionally, the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) map reveals that Ti element was evenly distributed on the PEA-PU-3T/M membrane surface, confirming the uniform distribution of TiO2/MCD photocatalyst. The elemental content images of PEA-PU-3T/M and PEA-3T/M were provided as further evidence to show their distribution on the membranes surface [66]. Notably, the elemental content of Al and Ti in PEA-PU-3T/M was 0.05 wt% and 2.76 wt%, respectively, which was higher than that in PEA-3T/M (0.04 % and 0.78 %), despite both membranes having the same TiO2/MCD dosage. This find indicates that higher-content TiO2/MCD hybrids were appeared on the surface of PEA-PU-3T/M. All these results firmly indicate the introduction PU facilitated the uniform dispersion of TiO2/MCD hybrids in PEA membrane.

The photodegradation mechanism of the prepared membranes was investigated through ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy. In Fig. 6
a, it is shown that both PEA-3T/M and PEA-PU-3T/M membranes displayed significant UV absorption (200–400 nm), while the latter showed enhanced absorption in both the UV and visible light regions. Meanwhile, Fig. 6 b shows that the band gap of PEA-PU-3T/M (2.86 eV) is slightly lower than that of PEA-3T/M (2.95 eV). This may be due to the addition of amphiphilic PU, which is more conducive to the homogeneous dispersion of TiO2/MCD, enabling it to occupy a larger membrane surface area, resulting sufficient available photocatalyst surface for incident light and consequently improvement of the light utilization. At the same time, the roughened membrane surface modified by PU and TiO2/MCD caused the light to undergo diffuse reflection, reflecting the incident light multiple times. This allowed the light to be absorbed at multiple levels on the surface, leading to enhanced photocatalytic performance of the membrane [67,68] (Fig. 6 c). In addition, the optical properties of photocatalytic membranes were investigated with varying TiO2/MCD hybrids content. As shown in Fig. S11 , PEA-PU-5T/M and PEA-PU-7T/M membranes exhibited an enhanced UV absorption (200–400 nm) with extended visible light response up to 600 nm and demonstrated bandgap energies of 2.83 eV and 2.80 eV, respectively. These values represented significant improvement over PEA-PU-3T/M membrane, confirming that increased TiO2/MCD hybrids contents effectively broadened the spectral absorption range and reduced bandgap energy [69]. Furthermore, as the increase of TiO2/MCD hybrids content, the photocatalytic activity of membranes enhanced, which was confirmed by the accelerated dye degradation rates shown in Fig. 4 a-b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sewage treatment performance of membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
After identifying the individual oil/water emulsion filtration and photocatalytic properties of these composite membranes, the performance of these membranes to treat more complexed dye-containing oil/water emulsion contaminant was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 7
a and Fig. S12 , alternating cycle separation tests for water and Rh B-containing oil/water- (oil–water-Rh B) emulsion were conducted to assess the pollutant-removal efficiency, stability and durability of the PEA-PU-3T/M and PEA-3T/M membranes. Throughout seven alternating cycle experiments, the water flux of the PEA-PU-3T/M membrane consistently remained above 790 L·m−2·h−1, while the flux of the oil–water Rh B emulsion stabilized around 280 L·m−2·h−1. However, during the seventh cycle, both fluxes exhibited a significant decline, with the oil–water emulsion flux decreasing to 194 L·m−2·h−1, indicating the onset of membrane contamination by oil and Rh B. Subsequently, the PEA-PU-3T/M membrane underwent UV irradiation for 10 min, followed by further cyclic separation test. Remarkably, the fluxes of the UV-irradiated membrane for both pure water and oil–water-Rh B emulsion were restored to their initial values. After subsequent three cycles, the fluxes remained the elevation at 784 L·m−2·h−1 for water and 270 L·m−2·h−1 for the oil–water-Rh B emulsion. However, the PEA-3T/M membrane exhibited a rapid decline in water flux, decreasing from 534 L·m−2·h−1 to 390 L·m−2·h−1 after four alternating cycle separation tests. The flux decrease of the oil–water-Rh B emulsion was even more pronounced. Although the membrane was exposed to UV irradiation for 10 min, the both fluxes recovery were minimal (only reaching 446 L·m−2·h−1 for water flux and 120 L·m−2·h−1 for emulsion flux). This also indicates that PU plays a crucial role in improvement of cycling performance for the PEA-PU-3T/M membrane. In short, as illustrated in Fig. 7 b, the contaminated PEA-PU-3T/M membrane, with decreased flux after cyclic filtration, allows for recovered flux through UV light-assisted photodegradation to clean the oil and dye pollutants [70]. Its stability and flux recovery were significantly higher than those of the PEA-3T/M membrane. Meanwhile, Fig. 7 c presents a comparative analysis of PEA-PU-3T/M and PEA-3T/M membranes, highlighting the superior performance of PEA-PU-3T/M in oil–water emulsion separation, photocatalytic activity, and cycling stability. These enhancements could be attributed to the amphiphilic ability of PU to augment membrane hydrophilicity and surface roughness, prevent TiO2/MCD aggregation, thereby optimizing light utilization and consequently bolstering photocatalytic properties. These features endowed the PEA-PU-3T/M membrane with desired photocatalytic-self-cleaning functionality. The optical images in Fig. 7 d distinctly show the two types of contaminated membranes after oil–water-dye emulsion separation and subsequently corresponding UV light-degraded cleaned membranes. Specifically, compared with polluted PEA-PU-3T/M, the contaminated PEA-3T/M exhibited a darker pink surface, indicating it suffered from a greater contamination. After a 10 min UV exposure, substantial contamination persisted on the surface of PEA-3T/M, whereas the PEA-PU-3T/M exhibited minimal residue. This highlights that the addition of amphiphilic PU not only enhanced anti-contamination properties of membrane, but also promoted TiO2/MCD to achieve superior photocatalytic performance in PEA-PU-3T/M membrane for effective degradation of oil and Rh B contaminants under UV light. The synergistic anti-fouling and photodegradation allowed the membrane with self-cleaning and enhanced durability. In addition, the photocatalytic dye degradation capacity of PEA-PU-3T/M membrane in extreme environments was investigated. The PEA-PU-3T/M membrane also showed desirable degradation efficiency for Rh B dyes under acidic and neutral conditions, with degradation rates of 90.1 %, 92.6 % and 99.3 % at pH 3, 5 and 7, respectively. However, a distinctly decreased Rh B degradation rates of 72.6 % and 81.2 % were observed at pH 9 and 11 ( Fig. S13
a). This decline might be related to the disruption of intermolecular hydrogen bonds in alkaline environment, which could impair the photocatalytic activity of the membrane. Additionally, the PEA-PU-3T/M membrane exhibited average Rh B degradation rate of 68.6 % and 54.7 % under high NaCl concentrations of 500 ppm and 1000 ppm, respectively ( Fig. S13
b). This significant reduction in catalytic efficiency was possibly attributed to the side reaction between radicals and anions in high salinity condition to quench ROS and consequently form weak radicals [71].

In previous studies, incorporating hydrophilic photocatalysts into highly hydrophobic polymeric substrates without sufficient interfacial interaction resulted in these composite membranes failed to significantly improve both hydrophilicity and photocatalytic efficiency (Table 2 ). As a result, their practical applicability in treating complex oily wastewater containing dyes would be severely limited. For example, most of them not only exhibited limited separation performance (Flux < 700 L·m−2·h−1, Rejection < 97 %, FRR < 92 %), but also needed an irradiation time (Irt) of more than 120 min under UV light to achieve the effective dyes degradation capability with a photodegradation ratio (Phr) > 97 %. In contrast, the optimized PEA-PU-T/M membranes developed in this study demonstrated a remarkable advancement in both separation efficiency (Flux = 968 L·m−2·h−1, Rejection = 98.8 %, FRR = 94.1 %) and photocatalytic activity (Irt = 40 min, Phr = 99.1 %). This enhanced performance positions the PEA-PU-T/M membranes as a highly promising candidate for treating complex wastewater containing oils and dyes.

In this work, a novel industrially-scalable functional polymer, polyarylene ether amidoxime (PEA), was synthesized to be used as membrane substrate. Then, amphiphilic PU and TiO2/MCD hybrids with photocatalytic activity were fabricated as additives to modify the pristine PEA membrane through one-step NIPS process. The resultant PEA-PU-T/M composite membranes obtained through a facile protocol exhibited long-lasting and high-efficient sewage treatment ability for dye-containing oil/water emulsions due to their synergistically enhanced anti-fouling and photocatalytic properties, which are originated from the PU-assisted uniform dispersion of TiO2/MCD hybrids in PEA membranes. Specifically, a series of characterization such as FT-IR, XPS and XRD were applied to characterize the chemical structure of these membranes, confirming their successful preparation. FT-IR results indicated that TiO2/MCD could uniformly disperse in the resultant membranes due to the hydrogen bonding interaction between PU and TiO2/MCD. The addition of the amphiphilic PU and hydrophilic TiO2/MCD increased the porosity, roughness and hydrophilicity of the membranes. Based on the improved pore structure and hydrophilic properties, the resultant PEA-PU-T/M membranes exhibited excellent water permeability and fouling resistance. Moreover, the PEA-PU-T/M membranes achieved removal rates of 94.2 % and 99.1 % for MB and Rh B, respectively, under 60 min UV light irradiation. This can be attributed to the excellent photocatalytic activity of TiO2/MCD hybrids, which were evenly dispersed in membranes under the assistance of PU. Additionally, the optimized membrane demonstrated excellent reusability in oil–water-dye emulsion filtration and photocatalytic performance. In summary, the PEA-PU-T/M membranes showed significant potential for water purification and photocatalytic self-cleaning, and can be used effectively in the treatment of textile wastewaters. Future research will prioritize the following three key areas: (1) developing visible-light-responsive photocatalytic systems to enable solar-driven water purification; (2) evaluating long-term stability under complex wastewater conditions; (3) optimizing the scalable fabrication technology of membranes to enhance compatibility with industrial wastewater processing.
